back_translation: In those days, when there was no king in Israel, a certain Levite was sojourning in the remote parts of the hill country of Ephraim, and he took for himself a concubine from Bethlehem in Judah.
But his concubine played the harlot against him (or: left him), and she went from him to her father's house at Bethlehem in Judah, and remained there for a period of four months.

Her husband rose and went after her to speak to her heart, to bring her back. His young servant was with him, and a pair of donkeys. She brought him into her father's house, and when the girl's father saw him, he was glad to meet him.
His father-in-law, the girl's father, detained him, and he stayed with him three days, and they ate and drank and lodged there.
On the fourth day they rose early in the morning, and he got up to depart, but the girl's father said to his son-in-law, 'Strengthen your heart with a piece of bread, and afterward you may go.'
So they sat down and the two of them ate and drank together. Then the girl's father said to the man, 'Please consent and lodge, and let your heart be merry.'
The man rose to depart, but his father-in-law detained him, so he returned and lodged there.
He rose early in the morning on the fifth day to depart, but the girl's father said, 'Please strengthen your heart and tarry until the day declines.' And the two of them ate.

The man rose to depart — he and his concubine and his young servant — but his father-in-law, the girl's father, said to him, 'Behold, the day has now weakened toward evening. Please lodge tonight. Behold, the day is coming to a close. Lodge here and let your heart be merry. Tomorrow you may rise early for your journey, and you may go to your tent.'
But the man was not willing to lodge. He rose and went and came as far as opposite Jebus (that is, Jerusalem), and with him was a pair of saddled donkeys, and his concubine was with him.
When they were near Jebus and the day had declined greatly, the young servant said to his master, 'Please come, let us turn aside into this city of the Jebusites and lodge in it.'
But his master said to him, 'We will not turn aside into the city of foreigners who are not from the sons of Israel here. We will pass on as far as Gibeah.'
And he said to his young servant, 'Come, let us approach one of these places, and let us lodge in Gibeah or in Ramah.'
So they passed on and traveled, and the sun set on them near Gibeah which belongs to Benjamin.
They turned aside there to enter and lodge in Gibeah, and he came in and sat in the city square, but no man took them into his house to lodge.
Behold, an old man was coming from his work in the field in the evening. The man was from the hill country of Ephraim, and he was sojourning in Gibeah, but the men of the place were Benjamites.
He lifted up his eyes and saw the traveling man in the city square, and the old man said, 'Where are you going, and where do you come from?'

He said to him, 'We are passing through from Bethlehem in Judah as far as the remote parts of the hill country of Ephraim — I am from there. I went as far as Bethlehem in Judah, and I am going to the house of the LORD, but no man takes me into his house.'
'There is both straw and fodder for our donkeys, and also bread and wine for me and for your maidservant, and for the young man with your servant. There is no lack of anything.'
The old man said, 'Peace to you. Only, let all your need be on me — only, do not lodge in the square.'
So he brought him to his house, and mixed fodder for the donkeys, and they washed their feet and ate and drank.

They were making their hearts merry, and behold, the men of the city — sons of Belial — surrounded the house, beating on the door. They said to the man, the owner of the house, the old man, 'Bring out the man who has come into your house, that we may know him.'
The owner of the house went out to them and said to them, 'No, my brothers, please do not do evil. After this man has come into my house, do not do this folly.'
'Behold, my virgin daughter and his concubine — let me bring them out now, and you humble them and do to them what is good in your eyes. But to this man do not do this thing of folly.'

But the men would not listen to him. So the man took hold of his concubine and brought her out to them outside, and they knew her and abused her all night until morning, and they sent her away as the dawn was coming up.
The woman came at the turning of the morning and fell at the door of the man's house where her master was, until it was light.

Her master rose in the morning and opened the doors of the house and went out to go on his way, and behold, the woman his concubine had fallen at the door of the house, and her hands were on the threshold.
He said to her, 'Get up, and let us go.' But there was no answer. So he put her on the donkey, and the man rose and went to his place.
He came to his house and took the knife and seized his concubine and cut her into twelve pieces according to her bones, and sent her throughout all the territory of Israel.

And it was that everyone who saw said, 'There has not happened and has not been seen such a thing as this from the day the sons of Israel came up from the land of Egypt until this day. Set your minds upon her, take counsel, and speak.'
